steps.prompt: Tilt the crossing line and watch the blue angles
steps.subtitle: Drag the handle to rotate the diagonal line. The two [corresponding angles](blue) are highlighted — see whether they stay equal as you tilt.

steps.prompt: Train your eye: make a 120° angle
steps.subtitle: Drag the slider until the angle looks like 120° — a bit wider than a square corner (90°). Trust your eyes first, then check.

feedback.incorrect: A square corner is 90°. 120° is clearly wider than that — open it a bit more or less.

steps.revealText: That's about 120° — an obtuse angle (wider than 90° but less than 180°). Getting a feel for sizes makes the rules click.

steps.body:
At any crossing: [vertical angles](purple) (straight across from each other) are equal, and two angles on a straight line add to 180°.
Across two parallel lines: corresponding angles (same corner at each crossing) are equal, [alternate interior angles](orange) (the Z-shape, highlighted) are equal, and co-interior angles (the C-shape) add to 180°.

steps.subtitle: Two parallel lines are cut by a transversal, and one angle is [70°](orange). Select every angle that has to be 70° too.

options.label: Its neighbour on the straight line

feedback.correct: Yes! Vertical, corresponding, and alternate angles all copy the 70°. The straight-line neighbour and the co-interior angle are 110° instead — they fill up to 180°.
feedback.incorrect: Equal pairs copy the angle; supplementary pairs make 180°. The straight-line neighbour and co-interior angle are 110°, not 70°.

steps.subtitle: Some angle pairs are always equal; others are supplementary, adding to 180°. Judge each claim about two parallel lines cut by a transversal.

cards.note: Yes — the angles straight across from each other at a crossing are always equal.
True — the Z-shape between the parallel lines holds two equal angles.
No — co-interior (C-shape) angles are supplementary: they add to 180°, not equal. If one is 70° the other is 110°.
True — the same corner at each crossing gives equal angles.

cards.statement: Two angles on a straight line add to 180°.
cards.note: Yes — a straight line is a 180° angle, so its two parts sum to 180°. That's why a 70° angle's neighbour is 110°.

steps.doneNote: Equal pairs: vertical, corresponding, alternate. Supplementary (180°) pairs: straight-line and co-interior.

steps.prompt: Proof: parallel lines make equal angles

gates.prompt: Slide the top crossing straight down onto the bottom one. Why does the corresponding angle match?

choices.label: The lines are parallel, so the picture lands exactly on itself.

gates.hint: Parallel lines make the two crossings identical copies.

gates.prompt: At the bottom crossing, the angle vertically opposite that 70° is…
That 70° and the first 70° are alternate interior angles. The 70°'s straight-line neighbour is…
gates.hint: Angles on a straight line sum to 180°: 180° − 70°.

stages: A transversal cuts two parallel lines. Mark one angle where it meets the top line — here it is 70°.
Slide that crossing straight down onto the bottom one. Because the lines are parallel, the picture lands exactly on itself, so the corresponding angle is also 70°.
At the bottom crossing, the angle vertically opposite is equal too — another 70°. It sits between the lines, on the far side of the transversal.
That 70° is the alternate interior angle of the first, so alternate interior angles are equal. Its neighbour on the straight line is 110°, so co-interior angles add to 70° + 110° = 180°.

problems.prompt: Two parallel lines are cut by a transversal. An interior angle is 50°. Find its alternate interior angle.

feedback.correct: Correct. Alternate interior angles (the Z shape) are equal: 50°.
feedback.incorrect: Alternate interior angles form a Z between the parallel lines, so they are equal.

problems.strongHint: Alternate interior angles are equal, so the answer is 50°.

problems.prompt: Two parallel lines are cut by a transversal. Two co-interior angles lie on the same side, between the lines. One is 115°. Find the other.
∠1 and ∠2 are vertical angles. ∠2 and ∠3 are corresponding angles where a transversal cuts two parallel lines. If ∠1 = 62°, find ∠3.

feedback.incorrect: Chase it one link at a time: vertical angles are equal, then corresponding angles are equal.

problems.strongHint: Each link keeps the angle the same, so ∠3 = 62°.

steps.subtitle: Fill in the words and numbers that capture your angle-chasing toolkit.
steps.template: The purple [vertical angles](purple) — straight across the crossing — are {{rel}}. Co-interior angles add to {{sum}}°, so if one co-interior angle is 70°, the other is {{other}}°.

feedback.incorrect: Vertical angles match each other. Co-interior angles are supplementary, so they total 180° — and 180° − 70° = 110°.

steps.doneNote: Vertical (and corresponding and alternate) angles are equal; co-interior angles sum to 180°.
